summary: I completed my undergraduate studies at Whitman College in Washington before pursuing graduate work. It was the foundation of my academic journey that led me eventually to roles in higher education administration. Whitman College provided me with the initial academic grounding that shaped my career in education.

Washington state is where I attended Whitman College for my undergraduate degree and later worked at the State College of Washington in Pullman. My sixteen years as a Dean of Women included time spent in Pullman, Washington, which was a significant part of my career before going to Japan. The state played an important role in my professional development in higher education.

After Whitman College, I pursued my Master's and Ph.D. degrees at Teachers College, Columbia University, specializing in Student Personnel work. This advanced training prepared me for leadership roles in higher education, including my later appointment with SCAP in Japan. Teachers College was instrumental in shaping my expertise in student affairs and education administration.

I served as Dean of Women at Drury College in Springfield, Missouri, for part of my sixteen years in that role. This position was an early leadership experience in my career, where I gained valuable insight into women's higher education. Drury was a key stepping stone before my work in Washington and later in Japan.

Springfield, Missouri, is where Drury College is located, and where I worked as Dean of Women before moving on to Washington. My time in Springfield was an important phase in my career, giving me experience in college administration and women's education. It was a significant place in my professional journey prior to my work overseas.

I was Dean of Women at the State College of Washington, which is now known as Washington State University, located in Pullman. This role was part of my sixteen years as a dean and was crucial in my development as an educator and administrator. My experiences there helped prepare me for my later advisory role in Japan.

Pullman, Washington, is where the State College of Washington is located, and where I served as Dean of Women. My work in Pullman was a significant part of my sixteen-year tenure in higher education administration. It was from this experience and my earlier work that I was able to transition to advising on women's education in Japan.

I spent a year on leave as a professor of history at Kobe Jogakuin University in Japan. During that time, I learned a great deal about the challenges of women's education in Japan under the guidance of Dr. Charlotte DeForest, the long-time president of the college. This experience was pivotal and led to my appointment as Adviser on Higher Education for Women for SCAP.

Dr. Charlotte DeForest was the long-time president of Kobe Jogakuin University, where I spent a year as a professor of history. Under her tutelage, I gained valuable insights into the problems of women's education in Japan. Her mentorship was instrumental in shaping my understanding and later work with SCAP.

entity: Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers
summary: I was appointed Adviser on Higher Education for Women within the Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers, or SCAP, when I reported for duty at Radio Tokyo on August 7th, 1946. SCAP had been operating since September 22nd, 1945, overseeing the Occupation of Japan. My role was part of the Civil Information and Education section, working to establish a democratic education system in postwar Japan.

I reported for duty at Radio Tokyo on August 7th, 1946, where the military section of SCAP was headquartered. This was the center of operations for the Occupation's Civil Information and Education section, where I was assigned. Radio Tokyo was a key location for coordinating educational reforms in postwar Japan.

Lieutenant Colonel Donald Dugent was the chief of the Civil Information and Education section at SCAP when I arrived. He had prior experience with Japanese schools before the war, which was invaluable to our work. I worked under his leadership as we sought to reform education in Japan.
cues: chief of Civil Information and Education

summary: Colonel Mark Orr was the head of the Education Division within SCAP's Civil Information and Education section. He was one of the key leaders alongside Lieutenant Colonel Dugent during the Occupation's efforts to reform Japanese education. I worked in the Education Division, collaborating with him and others to implement changes.

Eileen Donovan was a WAG and former junior high school teacher from Boston who worked with me on women's education in Japan. Together, we focused on developing educational opportunities for women during the Occupation. Her background in education was a great asset to our team.

In March 1946, an Education Mission from the United States, consisting of thirty prominent university professors and administrators, visited Japan for one month. They made recommendations on reforming Japan's educational system, which guided our work at SCAP. Their findings, along with the Japan Education Reform Committee's input, shaped the direction of educational changes.

Shigeru Nombara was the chairman of the Japan Education Reform Committee and president of Tokyo Imperial University at the time. His committee worked alongside the American Education Mission to provide recommendations for Japan's educational reforms. We used their guidance extensively in our planning and collaboration with Japanese officials.

The University of Tokyo, then called Tokyo Imperial University, was led by President Shigeru Nombara during the postwar period. The university played a central role in Japan's education system and reform efforts. Through President Nombara's leadership, the university contributed to the Education Reform Committee's recommendations that guided our work.

entity: Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology
summary: The Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology, known as the Mombusho, was the federal ministry controlling Japan's entire education system. I worked closely with officials from the Mombusho to implement educational reforms. The ministry had strict control over all levels of education, from primary schools to universities.

General Douglas MacArthur was the Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers who directed that all changes in Japan's government be made through existing Japanese officials. His directive shaped how we worked with the Ministry of Education and other Japanese authorities. MacArthur's leadership was central to the Occupation's approach to reform.

Dr. Tatsuo Morita was the Minister of Education for most of the time I was in Japan. As head of the Mombusho, he played a key role in overseeing the education system during the Occupation. I worked with his ministry to help implement reforms in higher education, especially for women.

Mr. Katsui was from the social education section of the Mombusho and worked closely with the Education Division of SCAP. I collaborated with him regularly to ensure that our recommendations aligned with Japanese policies. His role was vital in facilitating communication and action within the ministry.

Mr. D. Hidaka was the chief of the Bureau of Higher Schools within the Mombusho and a key contact for the Education Division at SCAP. I worked directly with him to coordinate reforms in higher education. His position allowed us to navigate the ministry's bureaucracy effectively.
cues: chief of Bureau of Higher Schools

summary: The National Diet was the legislative body in Japan to which educational reforms sometimes had to be sent for approval. Working through the Mombusho, proposals for change were often forwarded to the Diet or the Minister for decision. The Diet was an important part of the process in establishing democratic education in postwar Japan.
